nodes.text: X — Wheel of Fortune

THE WHEEL OF FORTUNE. It turns. It has always been turning. The sphinx at the top does not look down. The figures on the wheel do not choose their position. The wheel is not cruel. The wheel is not kind. The wheel is simply the wheel. Reversed: bad luck that was always luck. The fall that was built into the rise.
The motion was denied.

Erica Campbell stared at the email notification, the crisp black sans-serif font glowing with indifferent finality on her monitor. Denied. Just like that. Six weeks of meticulously crafted arguments. Late nights fueled by lukewarm coffee and the acidic tang of adrenaline. Precedents cross-referenced until her eyes burned, and the burn had begun to require its own eyedrop, which she now kept in the third drawer of her desk beside the spare phone charger and the small plastic comb her mother had given her in 2003 and which she had not, in twenty-three years, replaced.
The motion. Six weeks. Reduced to a single dismissive digital verdict.

The ground, which had felt suspiciously unsteady all week, gave another nauseating lurch beneath her ergonomic chair. She gripped the armrests. The lurch was, technically, internal. The lurch was her own vertebrae registering an objection her mind had not yet authorized. She held still until the lurch resolved, then breathed out slowly through her teeth, the way her physical therapist had taught her to breathe in 2019, the year she had first noticed that her body had begun keeping a separate set of books from her schedule.
This was the gamble, wasn't it.

The daily roll of the dice in the hallowed, hermetically sealed halls of corporate law. High stakes. Immediate peril. Reputations balanced on the knife-edge of a well-placed comma or a strategically omitted clause. Erica thrived on it, usually. The precision. The logic. The intellectual combat. It was a bulwark against the messy unpredictable chaos of — well, everything else.
Lately the bulwark felt like it was built on shifting sand.

She closed the email. She did not, as she had once, in her twenties, made a habit of doing, write a brief defiant memo to herself about the ruling and what it indicated about the judge. The memos had not, in retrospect, helped. The memos had simply documented her annoyance for the benefit of a future Erica who would not need the documentation, because the future Erica had already inherited the annoyance free of charge.
She stood up. She walked to the glass wall of her office and looked out at Houston.
Her office was a temple to control.

Glass walls. Chrome accents. Files indexed with obsessive precision, paper and digital, the digital backed up nightly to two separate clouds and the paper rotated quarterly into the firm's secure archive on the seventeenth floor. Black and white. Plaintiff and defendant. Liable and not liable. Clear lines, defensible positions, every contract she touched a small architectural object she could walk around in her mind and inspect for load-bearing weaknesses before signing her name to it.
Except the lines were blurring everywhere else.

Outside her window, Houston in late afternoon: the freeway traffic doing its slow desperate ballet, the heat-shimmer rising off the asphalt twelve stories below, a single bird circling on a thermal in a way that looked, from this angle, like the bird had not yet decided whether to commit to the thermal or peel away. The bird was, Erica was almost certain, a hawk. Hawks did not, in her experience, indecide.
She watched the bird for longer than she had meant to. The bird, eventually, peeled away.
Take Alberto D'Ambrosio.

Calling again. Demanding. Confiding. Pulling her back into that Graustark orbit she thought she had escaped after his father's death — except his father wasn't dead, was he, his father was at this very hour drinking bourbon on a riverboat that did not, technically, sail anywhere, and the after his father's death phrasing had become Alberto's preferred euphemism for after my father stopped recognizing my authority, which was a different threshold and had been crossed, Erica understood, sometime in the spring of 2023.

She had been Alberto's childhood friend. She had been, then, his condolences-offerer when the riverboat first started losing money in 2020. She had been, then, his informal counselor when he began the long careful process of building his own Houston version of the family business, a process that had required, among other things, that she draft seven separate non-disclosure agreements for vendors and contractors who would be working on the build-out of D'Ambrosio's: Houston without being told whose money was, ultimately, behind it.
She was now — she still wasn't quite sure what she was now. The category had drifted in the past nine months in a direction she had not authorized.
He was a calculated risk she hadn't fully assessed. A variable throwing off her carefully balanced equation. A ghost from a past life demanding present consideration.

He had called twice yesterday. He had called once already this morning. He would call again before five. The pattern was the message. The pattern said I require you to be available. The pattern said I am not, technically, asking.

She had not yet decided whether the pattern was being directed at her in her capacity as a friend, or in her capacity as a lawyer, or in her capacity as something else, and the not-having-decided was, itself, a piece of information she had not yet metabolized.
Then there was the Artist Formerly Known As Her Boyfriend.

The ex-NASA programmer turned painter of screaming chaotic canvases, who had quit JPL in 2017 after a disagreement she had never asked him to fully explain, and who now lived in a converted warehouse in the Heights and made paintings of which the smallest was eight feet square and the largest, the one that had been on the cover of Texas Monthly last year, was the size of a parking spot. He represented the other kind of peril. The emotional, unpredictable kind.
He'd call at three AM. Talking about fractal consciousness. The hidden geometry of despair. The kind of three-AM call that, if she were a different woman, she would have stopped picking up by now.

She still picked up. He knew she still picked up. The picking up was not, anymore, a courtesy. The picking up was a statement she was trying to figure out the meaning of in real time, every time the phone rang.
He was exhilarating. He was exhausting. He was the antithesis of the ordered world she craved and, terrifyingly, the messy truth she felt increasingly drawn toward.
Logic dictated she cut ties.
Her gut, lately, was staging a hostile takeover. Her gut was filing motions her mind had not authorized. Her gut was, on present trends, going to win.
She turned away from the window. Walked back to the desk. Sat down.

She ran a hand over the smooth cool surface of the desk. The desk was teak, custom-built, paid for in 2014 with the bonus from her first full-equity year at the firm. It had cost her, at the time, the equivalent of two months' salary. It had not, in twelve years, developed a single visible imperfection. She had begun, in recent months, to find this fact slightly oppressive.

Control. That was the illusion she sold herself, sold her clients. She navigated the treacherous currents of contract law, anticipating breaches, mitigating damages, constructing arguments solid as reinforced concrete.
Like the closing arguments in Newsom v. PetroTex.

Standing before that jury, the weight of Graustark's grief pressing down, weaving facts and precedents into an unassailable case against corporate negligence. They must pay for what they've done. We seek not vengeance, but justice. She had won. A unanimous verdict. The settlement that followed had been the largest of her career, and the photograph of her on the courthouse steps afterward — exhausted, triumphant, holding the hand of Mrs. Romero, who had lost her husband and two of her three sons in the chemical incident the case had been ostensibly about — had been the photograph the firm now used in its recruitment materials, slightly cropped to exclude Mrs. Romero, whose participation in the recruitment narrative had not, technically, been consented to.

Erica had asked, at the time, that they leave Mrs. Romero in. The firm had said yes. The marketing department had quietly cropped Mrs. Romero out of the version that went into the brochure four months later, and Erica had not, when she noticed, made an issue of it, because by then she had already been working on the next case and had needed the recruiting brochure to function, and functioning, she had decided, was more important than fidelity.
She had told herself this at the time. She still told herself it.
It still rang, faintly, like a glass that has been struck and is waiting to crack.
A victory built on logic. Evidence. Righteous certainty. Black and white.
So why did everything feel so — gray.

Why did life outside these glass walls feel like a roulette wheel spun by a cackling indifferent god. Destiny. Cycles. Turning points. She hated the woolly thinking of it, the lack of defined terms and conditions, the vocabulary borrowed wholesale from a generation of self-help books she had never finished reading and from a mother who had, in her later years, taken up tarot in the small Catholic-guilty way that Erica's mother had taken up most things.
She could not, she now realized, shake the feeling that she was caught in a current. Pulled by forces far beyond Section 3, Subsection A of any contract she had ever drafted.

She pulled up the PetroTex settlement agreement on her monitor. Scanned the dense paragraphs. It was airtight. A masterpiece of legal architecture. Section by section it had been built to withstand challenge from any direction the architecture itself could anticipate. The architecture could not, by definition, anticipate the directions it could not anticipate.

The faces of the plaintiffs flashed behind her eyes. Mrs. Romero clutching that photograph. Douglas Forte's simmering silent rage at the back of the courtroom — Forte had not been a plaintiff, had not been called as a witness, had been there because he had grown up next door to one of the Romero boys and had driven up from New Orleans to sit in the back row in the suit his mother had bought him for his confirmation, and had said nothing, the entire trial, to anyone except the bailiff, to whom he had said thank you once a day, on entry.
Justice served. Or just another move in a bigger more complex game, the consequences rippling outward in ways no document could ever fully contain.

She had won. The plaintiffs had been compensated. The defendant corporation had restructured. The chemicals, in some sense, had not stopped, because the chemicals were a category and not a single entity, and the category had simply migrated. Someone she did not know was, this afternoon, in a different town, beginning to build a different settlement that would, in eight years, look very much like hers.
Was that justice, exactly. Or was it the wheel.
Erica did not, today, have the energy to argue with herself about the distinction.
The phone rang.
She looked at it. She knew before she looked. The caller ID glowed: ALBERTO D.
High stakes. Reputation gambling. Immediate peril.
She took a breath. Smoothed her already immaculate suit jacket. The wheel was spinning. Time to place her bet.

She squared her shoulders. The sharp-minded lawyer clicked back into place — the way the sharp-minded lawyer had been clicking into place, on demand, in this office, for fourteen years, the click as automatic now as her morning coffee, as the four small adjustments she made to her hair before any deposition, as the precise breath she took before any closing.
She picked up the phone.
Alberto.
Eri. I need a favor.

His voice. Houston transposed onto Graustark, or vice versa, the same flat affect Dante had at the bourbon-and-bargaining hour, except Alberto did not drink bourbon and did not, in Erica's experience, bargain. Alberto issued. The issuing was the bargain.
Tell me.
I need you to look at a contract for me.
Send it over.
It's not that kind of contract.
She paused. The word contract, in Alberto D'Ambrosio's mouth, on a Friday afternoon, not that kind, meant something very specific, and she had a sudden cold clear sense of what it meant.
Alberto.
Hear me out.
Alberto. If this is about your brother, the answer is no.
A long pause on the other end. The kind of pause that was not, for once, calculated. The kind of pause that was somebody adjusting, in real time, to the fact that they had been read.
How did you know it was about Antonio.

Because everything's about Antonio with you. And because two people I trust have called me this week to ask, separately, whether anything strange was happening in New Orleans. And because Q. Paul has called your office three times in the last five days, according to your assistant, who I had a perfectly innocent lunch with on Tuesday, and you do not need me to enumerate the inferences from there.
Another pause. Then a small dry laugh — the laugh that, in Alberto, was the closest thing to admiration he ever let surface.
Eri. You should be running this firm.
I am, effectively.
Yeah. You are. Listen. Antonio's about to make a decision. I want to be in a position to help him after he makes it. Not before. Before, he wouldn't take help.
And you want me to —
I want you to draft the help. So that when he calls — and he will, eventually — there's something on the table he can sign without us having to negotiate.
Alberto. He has a lawyer.
Antonio's lawyer is Jimmy Daigle's accountant's brother-in-law. He's not a lawyer Antonio chose. He's a lawyer Jimmy assigned.
Erica was quiet for a moment. The implications were, suddenly, stacked four deep, and she could see the bottom of the stack.
How much trouble is he in.
Not yet enough.
Alberto.
Eri. Not yet enough.

She thought about the wheel. She thought about Mrs. Romero at the back of the courtroom, hands folded, the hands that had been folded for twelve straight days of testimony without ever, that Erica had observed, separating. She thought about Douglas Forte in the back row in his confirmation suit. She thought about the painter who would call her at three AM tonight to talk about fractal consciousness, and who would, when she did not pick up — because tonight, she suddenly knew, she was not going to pick up — leave a voicemail she would, for the first time in a year, listen to in full.
Send me what you have.
Tonight?
Tonight.
Eri. Thank you.
Don't thank me, Alberto. I haven't done anything yet.
You will.
Maybe.
The line went quiet. Then, in the smaller voice he used when he was being, by his own austere standards, sincere:
I appreciate it, Eri. It matters.
Goodbye, Alberto.
She hung up.
She sat at the teak desk for a long time.

The Houston light shifted through the glass wall, going from afternoon-gold to the particular bruised amber that meant the bird she had watched earlier had long since landed, that the freeway twelve stories down was now full of people driving home to evenings she could not, from this angle, imagine.
The motion, on her monitor, was still denied. She had not closed the email. The email continued to be denied, continuously, in the way emails do.
She thought, the wheel is spinning.
She thought, I have been on the wheel for some time, and I have been telling myself I was the one spinning it.
She thought, the painter is going to call at three AM and tonight I am going to listen.

She picked up the phone. She thumbed past Alberto's number. She found the painter's. She did not, even now, have him saved under his name. He was saved under J — for Jordan, his actual first name, which she had stopped using when they began sleeping together, on the theory that calling him Jordan would make the sleeping-together feel like a relationship and that not calling him Jordan would keep it from feeling like one. The not-calling-him-Jordan had not, as it turned out, kept it from feeling like one. The not-calling-him-Jordan had simply made the feeling-like-one feel like a betrayal of her own design.
She thumbed his contact.
She did not press call.
She set the phone down.

She crossed back to the glass wall. The Houston dusk was now full color. The city was moving through its slow rich evening transitions, from the working day to whatever came after. Somewhere, in one of the buildings at the edge of her sightline, a light came on in an office she did not recognize.
Erica watched the light for a long moment.

Then she turned away from the window, walked back to the desk, sat down, and began, with the small careful precision that was her actual gift — not the certainty, which she had been mistaking it for, but the precision — to draft the kind of contract Antonio D'Ambrosio would, in eight weeks, find himself in a position to need.
The wheel kept spinning.
She, for once, allowed it to.

The light on the desk made small reflections in the chrome and the glass and the smooth teak. The denied motion sat unresolved on the monitor, awaiting the next morning's reply. The painter's call would come at three AM, as predicted. She would, as predicted, listen.
The house, this time, might not win.
She was, she realized, already counting on it.
